problem_summary: 2018 MCM Problem A (continuous): model HF (3-30 MHz) sky-wave radio propagation that "hops" by reflecting between the ionosphere (F2 layer) and the Earth's surface. Tasks: (1) compute the strength of the first reflection off a turbulent ocean vs a calm ocean; (2) determine the maximum number of hops before SNR drops below a usable threshold; (3) compare results for reflections off different land topography vs the sea; (4) adapt the model to a shipboard receiver moving on a turbulent ocean. This team (control 86103) built a simulation-driven loss-budget model and answered all four parts with concrete numbers: 6 hops over calm sea, 4 hops over flat ground, ~3000 km between path changes for a moving ship.
models_used: Longuet-Higgins random wave model: sea surface as superposition of countless random cosine waves with random frequency/direction/phase, extended to a 3-D irregular short-peak wave field (double sum over frequencies i and directions j) — eqs (1),(2),(4)
Pierson-Moskowitz (PM) ocean wave spectrum S(omega) driving the wave amplitudes as a function of wind speed v at 19.5 m — eq (3); wind 'scale' 0-8 is the single environmental knob
Decibel loss-budget decomposition: Lb = Lbf (free-space) + La (ionospheric absorption) + Lg (surface reflection) + Yp (additional) — eq (5), the organizing spine of the whole paper
Free-space loss Lbf = 32.44 + 20·lg f + 20·lg r (Friis-type) — eq (6); plus max single-hop geometry Dmax from Earth radius Re=6371 km and F2 virtual height (~4000 km/hop) — eq (7)

Fresnel reflection coefficients for horizontal/vertical polarization (RH, RV) over a lossy dielectric sea, with complex permittivity epsilon~ = epsilon - j·60·lambda·sigma; reflection loss for mixed polarization Lg = 10·lg((RV^2+RH^2)/2) — eqs (10)-(13)

Custom geometric-optics 'roughness correction' for the turbulent sea: differentiate the wave cross-section xi(x), trace each micro-facet tangent/reflected ray via Snell's law, count rays reflected in the forward direction to get correction factor rho = m/M; add a shadowing correction rho' = rho·(S-s)/S for wave-occluded surface — eqs (14)-(18). This is the paper's signature original contribution.
Ionospheric D-layer absorption model La with sunspot number R and solar zenith angle chi (absorption index I) — eqs (8),(9), though ultimately fixed at 4 dB for tractability

SNR link budget combining HF sky-wave field intensity Et — eq (20) and atmospheric-noise field intensity En (CCIR Report 322 / lightning noise, table-lookup) — eq (21), giving closed-form SNR — eq (22)
Multi-hop accumulation laws: r = n·Dmax, La' = n·La, Lg' = (n-1)·Lg — eqs (23)-(25), solved for n with MATLAB fsolve
Moving-receiver extension: treat emission angle Delta, frequency f, power, and hop count n as free variables subject to SNR >= 10 dB; max time on one path tmax = r/v — eqs (26),(27)
key_techniques: Reduced an intractable rough-surface electromagnetic scattering problem to a 1-D ray-tracing count over a differentiated random wave profile (rho = forward-reflecting facets / total facets), avoiding full Kirchhoff/physical-optics integrals while still capturing the wind-dependent reflection drop

Multiplicative coupling of two sub-models: the calm-sea Fresnel coefficient is multiplied by the roughness correction factor rho' to get the turbulent-sea coefficient — clean separation of the smooth-surface physics from the sea-state physics

Reused the SAME 3-D wave model for land by reinterpreting wave fluctuation as terrain relief and swapping in land permittivity/conductivity (epsilon 3-8, geodetic sigma) plus a diffraction argument — answered the topography comparison with almost no new machinery
Aggressive, explicitly-justified constant approximations to keep the budget solvable: La fixed at 4 dB, Yp at 15 dB, permittivity/conductivity held constant — each flagged with a reason
Decibel additivity turned a chain of multiplicative physical losses into a linear sum, making both the n-hop accumulation and the fsolve root-find trivial
Database/table-lookup substitution for the 72-chart CCIR-322 atmospheric noise data instead of re-deriving it
Driving every figure from real MATLAB code (wave surface via PM spectrum, Fresnel curves, correction-coefficient curves, SNR mesh) included verbatim in the appendix for reproducibility
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it won because it converted a vaguely-specified physics prompt into a single, auditable decibel loss budget (Lb = Lbf + La + Lg + Yp) and then answered every sub-question with a concrete number traceable through that budget: first-reflection intensity vs wind scale (Fig 10), 6 hops over calm sea, 4 over flat land, and a ~3000 km/path-change result for the moving ship. The standout is the original turbulent-sea reflection treatment — rather than quoting an empirical roughness formula, the team built the PM-spectrum/Longuet-Higgins wave surface and ray-traced micro-facets (rho = m/M) plus a wave-shadowing correction, then multiplied that into the Fresnel coefficient. That is genuine modeling, quantitative, sea-state-dependent, and visibly their own (they call it out as a strength). It also closed the loop the prompt demanded: a direct sea-vs-land comparison from one shared model, and a moving-receiver extension with tmax = r/v. Reproducibility is high — real MATLAB for every figure — and assumptions are stated with reasons. The throughline from physical mechanism to wind/topography knob to final hop count is exactly the auditable, decision-relevant story judges reward."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet is a true tri-part summary that states the numeric answers up front (6 hops calm sea, 4 hops land, frequency-selection guidance), so a judge knows the conclusions before reading. Notation table and an explicit Assumptions section (each assumption paired with a 'Reason:') front-load the contract. The loss budget is introduced with a mermaid-style flowchart (Fig 4) that mirrors the section structure 4.3.1-4.3.4, so the document architecture = the model architecture. Figures carry the argument: wind-scale vs first-reflection-intensity bar charts (Figs 2,10), Fresnel RH/RV vs incidence-angle curves (Fig 5), correction-coefficient families vs wind speed/grazing angle (Figs 8,9), and a distance-frequency-SNR 3-D mesh (Fig 13). The sea-vs-land comparison is its own section (5) with a side-by-side first-reflection table and the n=4 result. A genuine Strengths/Weaknesses section honestly lists the simplifications (sky-wave only, fixed epsilon/sigma, refraction/diffraction ignored). Full runnable MATLAB appendix backs every figure."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Notation/labeling errors that survived to the final paper: eq (19) swaps the definitions of P0 and P1 (it says P1 is the transmit power and P0 the first-reflection intensity, the reverse of the notation table); the Table-1 parameter list gives f = 100 MHz although the model is explicitly HF (3-30 MHz) and uses 15 MHz elsewhere — almost certainly a typo for 100 W power

Over-reliance on hard-coded constants undercuts rigor: La=4 dB and Yp=15 dB are simply asserted, and epsilon/sigma held fixed — so the absorption and additional-loss terms, which dominate the budget, are essentially not modeled, only the reflection term Lg is

The signature rho = m/M facet-counting correction is described qualitatively but the actual counting/geometry algorithm is never fully specified or validated against any reference scattering model (e.g. no comparison to a known rough-surface RCS), so its quantitative accuracy is unverified

Internal inconsistency in hop accounting: eq (24) in the SNR section writes Lg' = (n-1)·Lg but the moving-ship eq (26) also uses (n-1)·Lg while the standalone eq (24) earlier in 4.5.3 is stated as Lg'=(n-1)Lg — fine — but La uses n·La; the reflection-count logic (n surface reflections vs n-1) is asserted, not derived

The ground model leans on a hand-wave: 'short wave wavelength is not enough to avoid obstacles so propagation is very weak' substitutes for an actual diffraction calculation, despite diffraction being listed as a modeled phenomenon
Numerous English-language and transcription slips (e.g. 'Sneel's law', 'layer 4 E or F', garbled appendix code with unbalanced parentheses) — cosmetic but indicate light proofreading
No sensitivity analysis or error bars: results like '6 hops' and '3000 km' are presented as point answers with no robustness study across the many fixed parameters
Appendix MATLAB contains transcription bugs (mismatched parens in rou2/rou3, '1ambda', truncated SNR line) so it would not run as printed
reusable_patterns: Decibel loss-budget spine: decompose any propagation/attenuation problem into additive dB terms (free-space + medium absorption + surface/reflection + residual), make that decomposition the section structure AND the figure structure via a flowchart — gives auditability for free

Spectrum-driven random-field surface generation: use a standard spectrum (PM, JONSWAP) + Longuet-Higgins superposition to synthesize a physically-plausible rough surface controlled by ONE environmental parameter (wind scale), then drive all downstream physics from it

Smooth-physics × roughness-correction factorization: compute the clean closed-form coefficient (Fresnel) and multiply by a separately-derived correction (here rho' from facet ray-tracing) — lets you reuse textbook formulas while still capturing the hard real-world effect

Cheap micro-facet ray-tracing as a surrogate for full-wave scattering: differentiate the surface, trace tangent/reflected rays per facet, count favorable outcomes (m/M) — a fast, defensible way to get a roughness loss without solving Maxwell's equations

Model reuse across sub-problems by reinterpretation: the same wave field served as terrain relief for the land comparison — one model, swapped material constants, answers two questions and creates a natural sea-vs-land narrative

Static-then-dynamic extension pattern: solve the fixed-receiver case first, then promote previously-fixed quantities (emission angle, n, frequency) to variables under a single constraint (SNR >= 10 dB) for the moving case, ending in a simple actionable formula (tmax = r/v)
Table-lookup / database substitution for unwieldy reference data (CCIR-322 noise charts) instead of re-deriving — pragmatic and clearly flagged
Assumption-with-reason formatting and an honest Strengths/Weaknesses section that names exactly which terms were approximated — buys credibility with judges
Ship every figure with the runnable code that produced it in the appendix to signal reproducibility
